**Toulouse Football Club** ([Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language"): *Tolosa Fotbòl Club*) is a French professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Toulouse](/wiki/Toulouse "Toulouse"). The club was founded in 1970 and currently plays in [Ligue 1](/wiki/Ligue_1 "Ligue 1"), the first division of [French football](/wiki/Football_in_France "Football in France"). Toulouse plays its home matches at the [Stadium de Toulouse](/wiki/Stadium_de_Toulouse "Stadium de Toulouse") located within the city.

*Les Violets* won the [Coupe de France](/wiki/Coupe_de_France "Coupe de France") in [2023](/wiki/2023_Coupe_de_France_final "2023 Coupe de France final"), and have won the second tier [Ligue 2](/wiki/Ligue_2 "Ligue 2") on three occasions.[[2]](#cite_note-tfclfp-2) Toulouse have participated in [European competition](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") six times, including in 2007 when they qualified for the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") for the first time.[[3]](#cite_note-toulousefc-3)

The current president of Toulouse FC is Olivier Cloarec, who was appointed following previous president [Damien Comolli](/wiki/Damien_Comolli "Damien Comolli")'s departure in July 2025.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Comolli succeeded the French businessman [Olivier Sadran](/wiki/Olivier_Sadran "Olivier Sadran"), who took over the club following its bankruptcy in 2001 which resulted in it being relegated to the [Championnat National](/wiki/Championnat_National "Championnat National"). The club has served as a springboard for several players, most notably the World Cup-winning goalkeeper [Fabien Barthez](/wiki/Fabien_Barthez "Fabien Barthez"), international strikers [André-Pierre Gignac](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9-Pierre_Gignac "André-Pierre Gignac"), [Martin Braithwaite](/wiki/Martin_Braithwaite "Martin Braithwaite") and [Wissam Ben Yedder](/wiki/Wissam_Ben_Yedder "Wissam Ben Yedder").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulouse_FC&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[The city](/wiki/Toulouse "Toulouse") was left without a big side in 1967 when [Toulouse FC](/wiki/Toulouse_FC_(1937) "Toulouse FC (1937)") sold its players and place in the French top flight to Paris outfit [Red Star](/wiki/Red_Star_F.C. "Red Star F.C."), but three years later a new club, Union Sportive Toulouse, rose from the ashes. Adopting red and yellow jerseys, the club started out in Ligue 2 and in 1979 reclaimed the name Toulouse FC. Now wearing purple and white, *Les Pitchouns* gained top-flight promotion in 1982. A side containing [Jacques Santini](/wiki/Jacques_Santini "Jacques Santini") and Swiss forward [Daniel Jeandupeux](/wiki/Daniel_Jeandupeux "Daniel Jeandupeux") earned a penalty shoot-out victory against [Diego Maradona](/wiki/Diego_Maradona "Diego Maradona")'s [Napoli](/wiki/S.S.C._Napoli "S.S.C. Napoli") in the [1986–87 UEFA Cup](/wiki/1986%E2%80%9387_UEFA_Cup "1986–87 UEFA Cup"), Toulouse's maiden European campaign.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Toulousef%C3%AAteTFC.JPG/250px-Toulousef%C3%AAteTFC.JPG)](/wiki/File:Toulousef%C3%AAteTFC.JPG)

Toulouse fans celebrate qualifying for the [2007–08 UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_UEFA_Champions_League "2007–08 UEFA Champions League")

After goalkeeper [Fabien Barthez](/wiki/Fabien_Barthez "Fabien Barthez") made his breakthrough and moved on, Toulouse were relegated in 1994. They subsequently bounced back and forth between Ligues 1 and 2 before slipping to the third flight in 2001 after financial problems. Toulouse were back in the top flight two seasons later, and in 2007 they finished third to earn a place in the [2007–08 UEFA Champions League third qualifying round](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_UEFA_Champions_League_qualifying_rounds "2007–08 UEFA Champions League qualifying rounds"). There, [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") overpowered them 5–0 on aggregate.[[3]](#cite_note-toulousefc-3)

In 2008–09, Toulouse finished fourth in the Ligue 1 table with 64 points, and secured a spot in the new Europa League, while [André-Pierre Gignac](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9-Pierre_Gignac "André-Pierre Gignac") led all scorers in Ligue 1 with 24 goals and was awarded a call-up to the France national team.[[6]](#cite_note-wiki-6)

In the 2015–16 [Ligue 1](/wiki/Ligue_1 "Ligue 1") season, Toulouse avoided relegation to [Ligue 2](/wiki/Ligue_2 "Ligue 2") in the last game of the season. With 12 minutes to go, Toulouse were behind to Angers 2–1 and needed a win to survive, and scored two late goals and won the match 3–2.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Two years later, they finished 18th and won the promotion/relegation playoff 4–0 on aggregate against Ligue 2's [AC Ajaccio](/wiki/AC_Ajaccio "AC Ajaccio").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

On 6 January 2020, Toulouse dismissed manager [Antoine Kombouaré](/wiki/Antoine_Kombouar%C3%A9 "Antoine Kombouaré") following the club's 1–0 loss to [Championnat National 2](/wiki/Championnat_National_2 "Championnat National 2") side [Saint-Pryvé Saint-Hilaire](/wiki/Saint-Pryv%C3%A9_Saint-Hilaire_FC "Saint-Pryvé Saint-Hilaire FC") in the [Coupe de France](/wiki/Coupe_de_France "Coupe de France"). Under Kombouaré the club had lost ten matches in a row, leading him to be dismissed and replaced by [Denis Zanko](/wiki/Denis_Zanko "Denis Zanko").[[9]](#cite_note-9) On 30 April that year, Toulouse were relegated to Ligue 2 after the LFP elected to end the season early due to the [coronavirus pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_France "COVID-19 pandemic in France").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On 21 July 2020, [RedBird Capital Partners](/wiki/RedBird_Capital_Partners "RedBird Capital Partners") acquired an 85% stake in Toulouse FC.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The club achieved promotion back to Ligue 1 by winning the second tier, [Ligue 2](/wiki/Ligue_2 "Ligue 2"), in 2022.[[12]](#cite_note-12) On 29 April 2023, Toulouse won its first-ever [Coupe de France](/wiki/Coupe_de_France "Coupe de France") title, defeating Kombouaré's [Nantes](/wiki/FC_Nantes "FC Nantes") in the [final](/wiki/2023_Coupe_de_France_final "2023 Coupe de France final") by a score of 5–1.[[13]](#cite_note-13) It was the city's second title as the former Toulouse FC had won it back in [1957](/wiki/1957_Coupe_de_France_final "1957 Coupe de France final").[[14]](#cite_note-:0-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulouse_FC&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Stadium")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Stadium-Lory.jpg/250px-Stadium-Lory.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stadium-Lory.jpg)

[Stadium de Toulouse](/wiki/Stadium_de_Toulouse "Stadium de Toulouse")

Main article: [Stadium de Toulouse](/wiki/Stadium_de_Toulouse "Stadium de Toulouse")

Toulouse play their home matches at the Stadium de Toulouse. Built in 1937, the stadium presently has a capacity of 33,150. The stadium was used as a venue for the [1998 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup"), [2007 Rugby Union World Cup](/wiki/2007_Rugby_Union_World_Cup "2007 Rugby Union World Cup") and [UEFA Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016").

## Colours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulouse_FC&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Colours")]

The violet is a reference to one of two Toulouse nicknames: *la Cité des violettes* (the City of Violets), the second one being *la Ville rose* (the Pink City), which explains the colour of former alternate jerseys.[[3]](#cite_note-toulousefc-3) The team's logo displays the gold and blood-red [Occitan cross](/wiki/Occitan_cross "Occitan cross"), the symbol of [Occitania](/wiki/Occitania "Occitania"), of which Toulouse is a historical capital.[[6]](#cite_note-wiki-6)

## Club rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulouse_FC&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Club rivalries")]

### Derby de la Garonne

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulouse_FC&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Derby de la Garonne")]

Main article: [Derby de la Garonne](/wiki/Derby_de_la_Garonne "Derby de la Garonne")

The Derby de la Garonne is a derby match between [Girondins de Bordeaux](/wiki/FC_Girondins_de_Bordeaux "FC Girondins de Bordeaux") and Toulouse. The derby derives from the fact that [Bordeaux](/wiki/Bordeaux "Bordeaux") and Toulouse are the two major cities in south-western France, both of which are situated on the [Garonne River](/wiki/Garonne "Garonne"). The consistency and competitiveness of the rivalry developed following Toulouse's return to [Ligue 1](/wiki/Ligue_1 "Ligue 1") after being administratively relegated to the [Championnat National](/wiki/Championnat_National "Championnat National") in 2001.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
